sections.heading: The Occasion: A Doxological Controversy
sections.html:
The Doxological Dispute

The immediate occasion of Basil's treatise was a controversy sparked by his own liturgical practice. In public worship at Caesarea he used two forms of the doxology: the traditional "Glory to the Father through the Son in the Holy Spirit" and the coordinate form "Glory to the Father with the Son together with the Holy Spirit." Critics, inclining toward Arian subordinationism, seized on the second form as an innovation, arguing that only the first, with its prepositions implying hierarchy and instrumentality, was warranted by Scripture and tradition.

Basil's response is methodical. He begins by noting that both forms had long and honorable use in the Church's prayer. The preposition "through" (dia) is indeed scriptural — Romans 11:36 and 1 Corinthians 8:6 speak of all things being through the Son — but it cannot bear the entire weight of Trinitarian doctrine by itself. The coordinate preposition "with" (meta, syn) expresses the Spirit's co-equal fellowship in the divine life, consistent with the baptismal command of Matthew 28:19.

Basil's Defense of Both Forms

Rather than abandoning either doxological form, Basil defends them as complementary. The "through" construction honors the order of Trinitarian relations — the Son as mediator through whom all things come to the Father, the Spirit as the sphere of divine action in which believers are sanctified. The "with" construction honors the equality of the three persons, resisting any suggestion that the Spirit is a subordinate instrument rather than a co-equal Lord.

Basil draws on 2 Corinthians 13:14, the apostolic benediction that coordinates the grace of Christ, the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit as three equal gifts flowing from one source. He also appeals to the seraphic doxology of Isaiah 6:3, whose threefold "Holy" the Church has always understood as directed equally to each person of the Trinity.

The Role of Tradition Alongside Scripture

One of Basil's most significant arguments is his appeal to unwritten tradition. He distinguishes between dogma (the Church's formal doctrinal deposit, much of which is delivered in unwritten form) and kerygma (publicly proclaimed teaching explicitly grounded in Scripture). Practices such as the sign of the cross, facing east in prayer, the epiclesis over the Eucharist, and the specific wording of doxologies have been transmitted without written mandate but with universal ecclesial consent.

This is not an elevation of tradition above Scripture but a recognition that Scripture itself is received and interpreted within a living community. 2 Thessalonians 2:15 commands believers to "hold to the traditions" delivered by the apostles, and 1 Timothy 6:20 exhorts the guarding of the deposit entrusted to the Church. The doxological tradition, Basil argues, is precisely such an apostolic deposit expressing the Church's lex orandi and therefore its lex credendi.

  • Rom. 11:36
  • Matt. 28:19
  • 2 Cor. 13:14
  • 2 Thess. 2:15
  • Isa. 6:3

sections.heading: The Deity and Personhood of the Spirit
sections.html:
The Spirit's Divine Titles and Works

Basil builds his pneumatology on a careful survey of the Spirit's scriptural titles and operations. The Spirit is called "Holy" — a designation shared with the Father and Son and indicating participation in the divine nature itself, not merely a moral quality conferred from without. He is called the Spirit of truth (John 16:13), the Spirit of wisdom (Isaiah 11:2), the Spirit of adoption (Romans 8:15), and the Spirit of glory (1 Peter 4:14) — each title transferring to the Spirit an attribute that Scripture elsewhere ascribes to God alone.

The Spirit's works are equally telling. He hovered over the waters at creation (Genesis 1:2), inspires all prophecy (2 Peter 1:21), raises the dead (Romans 8:11), distributes spiritual gifts sovereignly as he wills (1 Corinthians 12:11), and is present everywhere simultaneously — a divine attribute that no creature can possess. Basil notes that where the Spirit is withheld, the soul is darkened; where the Spirit indwells, the soul is illumined and conformed to God.

Lord and Life-Giver

Basil is careful in his choice of language, reflecting the theological climate of 375 when explicit statements of the Spirit's deity could provoke further schism. Yet the weight of his argument is unmistakable: the Spirit is Lord. 2 Corinthians 3:17 states directly, "The Lord is the Spirit," and the same passage speaks of transformation into the Lord's image by the Spirit of the Lord — a work only divine agency can accomplish. The Nicene formula, soon to be expanded at Constantinople (381), would enshrine the phrase "Lord and Life-giver" as the Church's formal pneumatological confession.

The Spirit as Life-giver echoes John 6:63 ("It is the Spirit who gives life") and Ezekiel 37:14 ("I will put my Spirit in you, and you shall live"). Basil observes that no creature can be the source of eternal life; to confer life in the fullest, eschatological sense requires a life that is underived and incommunicable — that is, divine life. The Spirit's life-giving work is therefore a direct proof of his deity.

The Baptismal Formula and the Spirit's Inseparability

The baptismal command of Matthew 28:19 — baptizing "in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" — is for Basil the strongest single piece of Trinitarian evidence. The use of the singular "name" (not names) coordinating all three persons implies a unity of being. One is not baptized into a committee of three divine beings but into the one name that Father, Son, and Spirit share. To rank the Spirit beneath the Father and Son is to fracture the baptismal confession and baptize people into something less than fully divine.

Basil reinforces this with the principle of inseparable operation. Father, Son, and Spirit never act in isolation from one another. In creation, in redemption, in sanctification, the three act as one — not sequentially or by division of labor in a way that would imply separability of essence, but in a single, undivided divine action expressed through distinct personal modes. 1 Corinthians 12:4–6 illustrates this: "the same Spirit… the same Lord… the same God" — three co-ordinate subjects of one undivided gift-giving activity.

  • John 16:13
  • Rom. 8:15
  • 1 Cor. 12:11
  • 2 Cor. 3:17
  • Matt. 28:19
  • John 6:63

sections.heading: The Spirit's Role in Salvation and Worship
sections.html:
Sanctification by the Spirit

Basil describes sanctification as the Spirit's proper and characteristic work. As the Father is the originating source and the Son the agent of redemption accomplished in history, the Spirit is the one who applies that redemption to individual souls, conforming them to the image of Christ. Romans 8:29 presents conformity to Christ's image as God's predestined goal for his people; 2 Corinthians 3:18 identifies the Spirit as the agent by whom this transformation is progressively worked.

This sanctifying work is not a secondary or optional divine activity but the very completion of salvation. Basil argues that without the Spirit's indwelling, the benefits secured by Christ's atoning death remain external to the believer. It is the Spirit who unites the soul to Christ (1 Corinthians 6:17), seals the believer unto the day of redemption (Ephesians 4:30), and produces the fruit of holy character (Galatians 5:22–23). The Spirit who sanctifies must therefore be no less than fully divine, for only God can make a creature genuinely holy.

The Spirit in Prayer and Prophecy

Basil gives particular attention to the Spirit's role in the Church's worship and prayer. Romans 8:26–27 teaches that the Spirit intercedes for believers with groanings too deep for words, searching the depths of God on behalf of the saints. This intercessory activity places the Spirit in an astonishing mediatorial position: within the divine life itself, the Spirit translates the believer's inarticulate desires into perfect petition before the Father. Only one who both knows the mind of God and indwells the human heart can perform this function.

Prophecy likewise belongs to the Spirit's domain. From the inspiration of the scriptural prophets (2 Peter 1:21) to the gifts exercised in the apostolic churches (1 Corinthians 14:1), the Spirit is the sovereign communicator of divine revelation. Basil sees the Spirit's role in prophecy not as mere verbal dictation but as an illumination of the human mind, enabling it to perceive and declare what eye has not seen and ear has not heard (1 Corinthians 2:9–10).

Calling the Spirit "God": Circumlocution and Confession

Basil concedes that Scripture rarely applies the bare title "God" directly to the Holy Spirit in the way it does to the Father and Son. His opponents exploited this silence. Basil's response is threefold. First, the absence of an explicit predication is not the same as a denial; Scripture also rarely calls the Father "God" in direct address in the New Testament, yet no one doubts the Father's deity. Second, Scripture uses functional circumlocutions — the Spirit as the one who searches the depths of God (1 Corinthians 2:10), who is poured out on all flesh (Acts 2:17), who is grieved by sin (Ephesians 4:30), and to lie to whom is to lie to God (Acts 5:3–4) — that collectively amount to a clear divine identification.

Third and most importantly, the Spirit's inclusion in the baptismal name, the doxology, and the apostolic benediction places him in an undeniably divine coordinate. The Church does not worship a hierarchy in which the Spirit is a subordinate minister; she worships the one God who is Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. To offer less than full doxological honor to the Spirit is, in Basil's view, to corrupt worship, weaken the baptismal confession, and ultimately undermine the entire structure of Trinitarian salvation — for a Spirit who is less than God cannot truly unite us to God.

  • Rom. 8:26–27
  • Rom. 8:29
  • 2 Cor. 3:18
  • Gal. 5:22–23
  • Acts 5:3–4
  • 1 Cor. 2:10
  • Eph. 4:30
